elements.sent-2411-002: We propose integrating an explicit approximation of a predictive control barrier function (PCBF) in a safety filter framework.
elements.sent-2411-003: The approximated PCBF is implicitly defined through an optimal control problem and allows guaranteeing invariance of an implicitly defined safe set as well as stability of this safe set within a larger domain of attraction.
elements.sent-2411-004: By extending existing theoretical analysis of the PCBF, we establish inherent robustness of the original algorithm and translate the guarantees to input-to-state stability of the proposed algorithm with respect to possible approximation errors, recovering the same guarantees in the absence of approximation errors.
elements.sent-2411-005: The proposed algorithm allows certifying inputs with respect to state constraint satisfaction through a single function evaluation and filtering unsafe inputs through a control barrier function based safety filter, which is independent of the time horizon of the original predictive optimisation problem, resulting in significant online computational benefits.
elements.sent-2411-006: We demonstrate the stability properties of the proposed algorithm on a linear system example as well as its use a fast safety filter for miniature race cars in simulation.
elements.sent-2411-007: Safety filters provide a modular mechanism which enables certifying, and if necessary modifying, any control input such that safety in terms of constraint satisfaction can be guaranteed.
elements.sent-2411-008: The filters are designed to be agnostic with respect to the main control performance goal and therefore aim to minimise the distance between the proposed input and the filtered input which is applied to the system.
elements.sent-2411-009: Thereby, safety filters are suitable tools to enhance the safety properties of various systems.
elements.sent-2411-010: The filters can be used to augment human agents operating systems, such as, e.g., automotive vehicles as demonstrated on miniature race cars in \cite{tearle2021 , and have also demonstrated their effectiveness in complex robotics applications, such as, e.g., walking on stepping stones with a legged robot in \cite{grandia2021 .}}
elements.sent-2411-011: The basic principle of safety filters consists in obtaining a control invariant set within the state constraints, such that inputs applied to the system guarantee that the system state remains within this invariant set.
elements.sent-2411-012: In order to obtain such a control invariant set, three main branches have been pursued, as discussed extensively in \cite{wabersich2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-013: Hamilton-Jacobi reachability analysis, see, e.g., \cite{chen2018 , allows computing the maximal control invariant subset to constraints, however the method suffers from poor computational scalability with respect to increasing state dimension.}
elements.sent-2411-014: Control barrier functions (CBFs), see, e.g., \cite{ames2019 for an overview, aim to alleviate the computational restrictions, by explicitly computing, potentially smaller, control invariant sets through either expert knowledge or more scalable computational methods compared to the Hamilton-Jacobi reachability, such as semi-definite programming or sum-of-squares optimisation, see, e.g., \cite{clark2021 .}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Finally, predictive safety filters have been proposed in \cite{wabersich2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-016: They employ model predictive control (MPC) techniques, see, e.g., \cite{rawlings2017 , in order to implicitly define a control invariant set through an online predictive optimisation problem.}
elements.sent-2411-017: These predictive methods make use of discrete time dynamics formulations and allow to easily provide recursive feasibility guarantees as well as extensions due to the well developed MPC theory to, e.g., robust treatments of disturbances, stochastic disturbances in \cite{wabersich21 or using online data for uncertain systems to improve safety filter performance in \cite{didier2021 .}}
elements.sent-2411-018: In practical systems, considering hard constraints in predictive control methods often leads to infeasibility due to unmodeled dynamics or sensor errors.
elements.sent-2411-019: Therefore, the state constraints on the system are often softened, as proposed, e.g., in \cite{kerrigan00 and used in \cite{tearle2021 , by introducing a slack variable.}}
elements.sent-2411-020: However, this leads in general to a loss of theoretical properties as soon as the system leaves the constraints.
elements.sent-2411-021: In \cite{wabersich2022 , a constraint softening mechanism is proposed, which is able to provide theoretical guarantees when constraints are violated.}
elements.sent-2411-022: Connections of this mechanism to CBFs are investigated and it is shown that by minimising a function which depends on the slack variables, a control barrier function is implicitly defined.
elements.sent-2411-023: This result implies that the implicitly defined safe set of a predictive safety filter is stable within a larger domain of attraction, such that the system is able to recover from unexpected disturbances or model mismatch.
elements.sent-2411-024: The main drawback of the method in \cite{wabersich2022 is the need to solve two predictive optimisation problems in order to guarantee stability, which results in a computationally demanding algorithm.}
elements.sent-2411-025: In this work, we extend initial results in \cite{didier2023 and aim to reduce the computational overhead of the predictive optimisation based PCBF method in \cite{wabersich2022 by obtaining an explicit approximation of the implicitly defined CBF.}}
elements.sent-2411-026: The explicit approximation can be used in a safety filter formulation similarly as in control barrier function based safety filters, such that only a single time step needs to be predicted using a decrease constraint on the value of the control barrier function.
elements.sent-2411-027: This decrease constraint further enables the introduction of a hyperparameter, which allows tuning the rate of stability decrease in closed-loop based on desired system behaviour.
elements.sent-2411-028: In comparison, in \cite{wabersich2022 the system is required to achieve the maximal possible decrease towards the implicitly defined safe set at every time step.}
elements.sent-2411-029: We further extend the theoretical results in \cite{wabersich2022 , by showing a rigorous connection between the control barrier function definition in \cite{wabersich2022 and more widely used class $\mathcal{K}$ comparator function based definitions.}}
elements.sent-2411-030: These relations allow to show that the method in \cite{wabersich2022 benefits from inherent robustness properties, see, e.g., \cite{allan2017 , which are crucial for deployment of predictive control methods in real world applications.}}
elements.sent-2411-031: By using the insights from the inherent robustness of the algorithm proposed in \cite{wabersich2022 , input-to-state stability of the proposed approximation-based algorithm is established with respect to the approximation errors.}
elements.sent-2411-032: This result provides a theoretical understanding of how approximation errors in the formulation potentially impact the closed-loop performance of the system.
elements.sent-2411-033: In addition, we propose a continuous extension of the PCBF, which is negative within the implicitly defined safe set.
elements.sent-2411-034: Thereby, approximation errors lead to a significant reduction in safety filter interventions when the system is safe compared to approximating the original PCBF, which is $0$ within the safe set.
elements.sent-2411-035: Finally, we show how the explicit approximation can be used in order to certify inputs using a single function evaluation, resulting in large computational speedups when the main controller proposes safe inputs.
elements.sent-2411-036: Safety filters have been extensively researched and a review of state-of-the-art methods can be found in \cite{wabersich2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-037: The first main branch of research consists of control barrier function based safety filters, originally proposed in \cite{wieland2007 for continuous time systems.}
elements.sent-2411-038: The work in \cite{agrawal2017 proposed discrete-time control barrier functions.}
elements.sent-2411-039: However, the proposed formulation uses a barrier function which approaches infinity as the state approaches the boundary of the safe set, which is not suitable for investigating the stability of the safe set within a larger domain.
elements.sent-2411-040: In \cite{chen2021 , a backup CBF formulation is proposed, which allows using a backup controller to enhance an invariant safe set, by computing gradients of a CBF on the closed-loop trajectory of the backup controller.}
elements.sent-2411-041: Thereby, any state which reaches the safe set under the backup controller is still considered safe, and the safe set is effectively augmented.
elements.sent-2411-042: The predictive safety filters in \cite{wabersich2021 and \cite{wabersich2022 show relations to this method, where the optimiser is free to choose all control inputs in the prediction horizon, rather than selecting them with a prior fixed policy.}}
elements.sent-2411-043: In \cite{didier2024predictive , the predictive safety filter is augmented with a stability guarantees of the origin using a Lyapunov decrease constraint.}
elements.sent-2411-044: Compared to these works, we combine the advantages of the existence of inputs which stabilise a safe set and render it invariant, while using a single CBF decrease constraint rather than a full prediction horizon, reducing the computational effort.

elements.sent-2411-047: Thereby, the control actions are modified such that safety can be guaranteed.
elements.sent-2411-048: However, this method does not consider the case when the input is constrained.
elements.sent-2411-049: Finally, \cite{wiltz2023 proposes a reachability-based safety filter formulation in continuous time, which makes use of a finite lookahead time and establishes connections to model predictive safety filter methods.}
elements.sent-2411-050: However, the method uses numerical approximations of the gradients of the CBF through sampling to impose the CBF decrease and it is unclear how approximation errors can impact the closed-loop performance.
elements.sent-2411-051: In comparison, we provide a rigorous analysis of how approximation errors of the learned CBF impact the closed-loop behaviour of the system.

elements.sent-2411-124: In this section, we provide the main theoretical analysis which provides the foundation of the approximation algorithm in Section~\ref{sec:apcbf .}
elements.sent-2411-126: We first show that any CBF according to Definition~\ref{def:CBFcont implies existence of a CBF according to Definition ~\ref{def:CBFclK .}}
elements.sent-2411-127: This result enables analysis of the closed-loop behaviour of the predictive CBF algorithm with respect to possible errors.

elements.sent-2411-336: We propose a computationally efficient safety filter formulation which relies on approximating the optimal value function of a predictive control method.
elements.sent-2411-337: This function encodes the information regarding safety in terms of state constraint satisfaction with respect to the entire prediction horizon of the predictive optimisation problem and enhances the safe set and domain of attraction of a terminal control barrier function.
elements.sent-2411-338: By using the explicit approximation, a horizon independent optimisation problem can be formulated with a single decrease constraint of the control barrier function.
elements.sent-2411-339: We advance the theoretical properties with respect to exogeneous disturbances of the original algorithm in \cite{wabersich2022 and translate the results to input-to-state stability of the proposed algorithm with respect to approximation errors.}
elements.sent-2411-340: Finally, we propose a negative continuous extension of the predictive control barrier function in order to mitigate approximation errors within the safe set.
elements.sent-2411-341: The theoretical properties are validated in an illustrative linear example and the practicality of a computationally advantageous safety filter is shown in a miniature race car simulation.
elements.sent-2411-342: In order to achieve a computationally efficient sampling of the extended PCBF $h$ in \eqref{eq:PCBFext , we propose a modification of the geometric sampling method presented in \cite{chen2022 in Algorithm~\ref{alg:sampling .}}}
elements.sent-2411-343: The geometric sampling method allows using previously computed solutions to the predictive optimisation problems for the computation of $h$ as a warmstart as well as storing information if the previous sample on a line search was within $\Spb$ or $\Dpb\setminus\Spb$, reducing the number of times two optimisation problems have to be solved.
elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{Sampling $h$}\label{alg:sampling} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \STATE Generate goal points $\mathbb{G}$ according to \cite[Algorithm 2]{chen2022} \STATE Initialise boolean $\mathrm{in\_}\mathcal{S}_{\mathrm{PB}}\leftarrow\mathrm{False}$ \STATE Choose initial seed $\mathbb{S}\leftarrow\{\mathbf{s}\} = \{ (x, \mathbf{o},\mathrm{in\_}\mathcal{S}_{\mathrm{PB}}) \}$ \STATE Initialise data set $\mathbb{D}\leftarrow\emptyset$ \STATE Choose step size $d>0$ \FOR{goal point $g\in \mathbb{G}$ (in parallel)} \STATE Sample seed $\mathbf{s}_0\leftarrow(x_0, \mathbf{o}_0, \mathrm{in\_}\mathcal{S}_{\mathrm{PB},0})\in\mathbb{S}$ \STATE Compute number of points on line $n\leftarrow\Vert x_0-g\Vert/d$ \FOR{$i=1, \dots, n$} \STATE $x_i\leftarrow x_0 + i (g-x_0)/n$ \IF{$\mathrm{in\_}\mathcal{S}_{\mathrm{PB},i-1}$} \STATE Obtain $\mathbf{o}_i$, $h(x_i)$ from \eqref{eq:optprobwithnegativeslacks} with warmstart $\mathbf{o}_{i-1}$ \IF{infeasible} \STATE $\mathrm{in\_}\mathcal{S}_{\mathrm{PB},i}\leftarrow$ False \STATE Obtain $\mathbf{o}_i$, $h(x_i)$ from \eqref{eq:pcbf} with warmstart $\mathbf{o}_{i-1}$ \ELSE \STATE $\mathrm{in\_}\mathcal{S}_{\mathrm{PB},i}\leftarrow$ True \ENDIF \STATE $\mathbf{s}_i \leftarrow (x_i, \mathbf{o}_i, \mathrm{in\_}\mathcal{S}_{\mathrm{PB},i})$ \STATE $\mathbb{D}\leftarrow \mathbb{D}\cup (x_i, h(x_i))$ \ELSE \STATE Obtain $\mathbf{o}_i$, $h(x_i)$ from \eqref{eq:pcbf} with warmstart $\mathbf{o}_{i-1}$ \IF{$\hpb(x_i)=0$} \STATE $\mathrm{in\_}\mathcal{S}_{\mathrm{PB},i}\leftarrow$ True \STATE Obtain $\mathbf{o}_i$, $h(x_i)$ from \eqref{eq:optprobwithnegativeslacks} with warmstart $\mathbf{o}_{i-1}$ \ELSE \STATE $\mathrm{in\_}\mathcal{S}_{\mathrm{PB},i}\leftarrow$ False \ENDIF \STATE $\mathbf{s}_i \leftarrow \{(x_i, \mathbf{o}_i, \mathrm{in\_}\mathcal{S}_{\mathrm{PB},i})\}$ \STATE $\mathbb{D}\leftarrow \mathbb{D}\cup (x_i, h(x_i))$ \ENDIF \ENDFOR \STATE $\mathbb{S}\leftarrow\mathbb{S}\cup \mathbf{s}_n$ \ENDFOR \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-353: We propose integrating an approximation of a predictive control barrier function (PCBF) in a safety filter framework, resulting in a prediction horizon independent formulation.
elements.sent-2411-354: The PCBF is defined through the value function of an optimal control problem and ensures invariance as well as stability of a safe set within a larger domain of attraction.
elements.sent-2411-355: We provide a theoretical analysis of the proposed algorithm, establishing input-to-state stability of the safe set with respect to approximation errors as well as exogenous disturbances.
elements.sent-2411-356: Furthermore, we propose a continuous extension of the PCBF within the safe set, reducing the impact of learning errors on filter interventions.
elements.sent-2411-357: We demonstrate the stability properties and computational advantages of the proposed algorithm on a linear system example and its application as a safety filter for miniature race cars in simulation.
elements.sent-2411-358: Safety filters provide a modular mechanism which enables certifying and, if necessary, modifying any control input such that safety in terms of constraint satisfaction can be guaranteed.
elements.sent-2411-359: The filters are designed to be agnostic with respect to the main control performance objective and therefore aim to minimise the distance between the proposed input and the filtered input which is applied to the system.
elements.sent-2411-360: The core principle of safety filters consists in obtaining a control invariant set within the state constraints, such that inputs applied to the system guarantee that the system state remains within this invariant set.
elements.sent-2411-361: Hamilton-Jacobi reachability analysis, as discussed in \cite{chen2018 , allows computing the maximal control invariant subset to constraints, however the method suffers from poor computational scalability with respect to increasing state dimension.}
elements.sent-2411-362: Control barrier functions (CBFs) aim to alleviate the computational restrictions, by explicitly computing, potentially smaller, control invariant sets.
elements.sent-2411-364: CBFs are obtained either through either expert knowledge or more scalable computational methods compared to the Hamilton-Jacobi reachability, such as semi-definite programming or sum-of-squares optimisation, such as in \cite{clark2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-365: They employ model predictive control (MPC) techniques in order to implicitly define a control invariant set through an online predictive optimisation problem.
elements.sent-2411-366: A detailed overview of such MPC techniques can be found in \cite{rawlings2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-367: These predictive methods make use of discrete-time dynamics formulations and allow to easily provide recursive feasibility guarantees.
elements.sent-2411-368: They enable extensions to, e.g., robust treatments of disturbances, stochastic disturbances in \cite{wabersich21 or using online data for uncertain systems to improve safety filter performance in \cite{didier2021 , due to the well developed MPC theory.}}
elements.sent-2411-369: In practical systems, considering hard constraints in predictive control methods often leads to infeasibility due to unmodelled dynamics or sensor noise.
elements.sent-2411-370: Therefore, the state constraints on the system are often softened, as proposed in~\cite{kerrigan00 and used in~\cite{tearle2021 , by introducing slack variables.}}
elements.sent-2411-371: However, this generally leads to a loss of theoretical properties as soon as the system violates the constraints.
elements.sent-2411-372: The main drawback of the method in \cite{wabersich2022 is the need to solve two predictive optimisation problems in order to guarantee stability, which results in a computationally demanding algorithm.. Contributions.}
elements.sent-2411-373: In this work, we leverage an explicit approximation of the PCBF in a safety filter formulation.
elements.sent-2411-374: This formulation requires predicting only a single time step ahead, allowing for efficient online computations.
elements.sent-2411-375: The contributions compared to initial results in~\cite{didier2023 are as follows:.}
elements.sent-2411-376: We extend initial input-to-state stability (ISS) analysis in~\cite{didier2023 , with respect to approximation errors, to the case of additional exogenous disturbances.}
elements.sent-2411-377: This is achieved through a relation between control barrier function (CBF) definitions based on continuity and class $\mathcal{K}$ comparison functions, which enables a general robust set stability analysis.
elements.sent-2411-378: The provided analysis directly implies ISS of the PCBF algorithm in~\cite{wabersich2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-379: We propose a novel continuous extension of the PCBF, leading to a significant reduction in safety filter interventions when the system is safe compared to~\cite{didier2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-380: We introduce a hyperparameter that reduces the required CBF decrease in closed-loop compared to~\cite{didier2023 and~\cite{wabersich2022 , while preserving stability guarantees.}}
elements.sent-2411-381: We show how the explicit approximation can be used in order to certify inputs using a single function evaluation, resulting in large computational speedups compared to~\cite{didier2023, wabersich2022 when safe inputs are proposed.}
elements.sent-2411-382: Due to the discrete-time formulation of the predictive controllers, the resulting CBF optimisation problem is a nonlinear program rather than a quadratic program in \cite{chen2021 , however the prediction horizon is not actually included in the online optimisation, effectively reducing the number of constraints.}
elements.sent-2411-383: Furthermore, if the prediction horizon of the predictive safety filter goes to infinity, the maximum control invariant set can be recovered, see, e.g., \cite[Definition 2.9]{kerrigan2000 .}
elements.sent-2411-384: Additionally, the required optimization problem is computationally complex, especially for neural network approximations.
elements.sent-2411-385: A discussion on loss function design for neural network approximations is provided in \cite{so2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-386: In comparison, we provide guarantees on closed-loop system behaviour even if approximation errors are still present in the learned CBF and additionally consider the effect of exogenous disturbances.
elements.sent-2411-387: Additionally, stability of the safe set itself is not considered in \cite{he2023 and recursive feasibility only holds if the approximation error is small enough, whereas the proposed formulation ensures feasibility everywhere.. Structure.}

elements.sent-2411-406: In this section, we review the concept of predictive safety filters, as proposed originally in~\cite{wabersich2018 and adapted in~\cite{wabersich2022 for system recovery, and how they can be related to the concept of CBFs.}}

elements.sent-2411-414: Such an enhancement property for the domain of attraction of the closed-loop system is achieved by computing the slack variables $\xi^*_{i|k}$ in \eqref{eq:pcbf_sf according to the following optimisation problem, which minimises the state constraint violations and a weighted penalty on the terminal slack $\xi_{N|k}$ with $\alpha_f>0$, i.e. \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\hpb(x(k)) := \min_{x_{i|k},u_{i|k}, \xi_{i|k}} \; \; \alpha_f \xi_{N|k} + \sum_{i=0}^{N-1}\lvert \lvert{\xi_{i|k}}\rvert \rvert \label{eq:cbf_pcbf} s.t.\; \; \forall\, i = 0,\dots,N-1, \nonumber x_{0|k} = x(k), \label{eq:slack_init_pred} x_{i+1|k} = f(x_{i|k}, u_{i|k}, 0), u_{i|k} \in U, \label{eq:slack_input_constraint} x_{i|k} \in \overline{X}_i(\xi_{i|k}), \; 0 \leq \xi_{i|k}, \label{eq:pcbf_state_constraint} h_f(x_{N|k}) \leq \xi_{N|k}, \; 0 \leq \xi_{N|k}.\end{equation}.}}

elements.algorithm-2411-003: \caption{Approximate PCBF safety filter}\label{alg:apcbf} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \FOR{$k\geq 0$} \STATE Measure state $x(k)$ \STATE Obtain the nominal control input $u_\mathrm{p}(k)$ \IF{$u_{\mathrm{p}}(k)\in\mathcal{U} \wedge \ahpb(f(x(k),u_\mathrm{p}(k)),0)\leq 0$} \STATE Apply $u(k)\leftarrow u_\mathrm{p}(k)$ // nominal input is safe \ELSE \STATE Compute $\Delta \ahpb(x(k))$ using~\eqref{eq:apcbf_maxdec} \STATE Solve~\eqref{eq:apcbf_sf} and obtain a solution $u^*$ \STATE Apply $u(k)\leftarrow u^*$ // apply filtered input \ENDIF \ENDFOR \end{algorithmic}

elements.algorithm-2411-004: \caption{Sampling $h_{CE}$}\label{alg:sampling} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \STATE Generate goal points $\mathbb{G}$ according to \cite[Algorithm 2]{chen2022} \STATE Initialise boolean $\mathrm{in\_}\mathcal{S}_{\mathrm{PB}}\leftarrow\mathrm{False}$ \STATE Choose initial seed $\mathbb{S}\leftarrow\{\mathbf{s}\} = \{ (x, \mathbf{o},\mathrm{in\_}\mathcal{S}_{\mathrm{PB}}) \}$ \STATE Initialise data set $\mathbb{D}\leftarrow\emptyset$ \STATE Choose step size $d>0$ \FOR{goal point $g\in \mathbb{G}$ (in parallel)} \STATE Sample seed $\mathbf{s}_0\leftarrow(x_0, \mathbf{o}_0, \mathrm{in\_}\mathcal{S}_{\mathrm{PB},0})\in\mathbb{S}$ \STATE Compute number of points on line $n\leftarrow\Vert x_0-g\Vert/d$ \FOR{$i=1, \dots, n$} \STATE $x_i\leftarrow x_0 + i (g-x_0)/n$ \IF{$\mathrm{in\_}\mathcal{S}_{\mathrm{PB},i-1}$} \STATE Obtain $\mathbf{o}_i$, $h_{CE}(x_i)$ from \eqref{eq:optprobwithnegativeslacks} with warmstart $\mathbf{o}_{i-1}$ \IF{infeasible} \STATE $\mathrm{in\_}\mathcal{S}_{\mathrm{PB},i}\leftarrow$ False \STATE Obtain $\mathbf{o}_i$, $h_{CE}(x_i)$ from \eqref{eq:pcbf} with warmstart $\mathbf{o}_{i-1}$ \ELSE \STATE $\mathrm{in\_}\mathcal{S}_{\mathrm{PB},i}\leftarrow$ True \ENDIF \STATE $\mathbf{s}_i \leftarrow (x_i, \mathbf{o}_i, \mathrm{in\_}\mathcal{S}_{\mathrm{PB},i})$ \STATE $\mathbb{D}\leftarrow \mathbb{D}\cup (x_i, h_{CE}(x_i))$ \ELSE \STATE Obtain $\mathbf{o}_i$, $h_{CE}(x_i)$ from \eqref{eq:pcbf} with warmstart $\mathbf{o}_{i-1}$ \IF{$\hpb(x_i)=0$} \STATE $\mathrm{in\_}\mathcal{S}_{\mathrm{PB},i}\leftarrow$ True \STATE Obtain $\mathbf{o}_i$, $h_{CE}(x_i)$ from \eqref{eq:optprobwithnegativeslacks} with warmstart $\mathbf{o}_{i-1}$ \ELSE \STATE $\mathrm{in\_}\mathcal{S}_{\mathrm{PB},i}\leftarrow$ False \ENDIF \STATE $\mathbf{s}_i \leftarrow \{(x_i, \mathbf{o}_i, \mathrm{in\_}\mathcal{S}_{\mathrm{PB},i})\}$ \STATE $\mathbb{D}\leftarrow \mathbb{D}\cup (x_i, h_{CE}(x_i))$ \ENDIF \ENDFOR \STATE $\mathbb{S}\leftarrow\mathbb{S}\cup \mathbf{s}_n$ \ENDFOR \end{algorithmic}
